title: Realm People & Custom
description: One terse gazetteer line on a realm's people, manners, and binding customs — for rollable realm entries.
entries: Guild-proud townsfolk; an oath sworn on iron is binding in law, and everyone carries a little iron for the purpose.
Herding clans who count wealth in cattle and insults in generations; hospitality is sacred for exactly three nights.
Riverfolk to the bone; every contract names a witness, a saint, and a boat.
Famously litigious; the smallest village keeps a court day, and watching trials is the national theater.
Ancestor-keeping; every house has a shrine shelf, and disinheriting someone means smashing their cup on it publicly.
Mercantile and unsentimental; the greeting is 'what do you carry?', and the answer is expected to be honest.
Old military families set the tone; retired sergeants run the schools, and posture is considered a moral quality.
Devout in a workaday fashion; the temples double as granaries, banks, and the only reliable archives.
Given to elaborate courtesy that visitors mistake for warmth; the forms matter more than the feeling, and both know it.
Frontier-minded even in the capital; fences are low, doors unlocked, and horse theft is the one unforgivable crime.
Scholars by reputation and clerks by fact; rank is by examination, and the exams are ferociously gamed.
Seafaring; a third of the realm is afloat at any hour, and drowned kin are spoken of in the present tense.
Winemakers and hosts; refusing a cup is refusing a friendship, which makes temperance a diplomatic skill.
Blunt to a fault; flattery is read as either mockery or a prelude to fraud.
Great keepers of festivals; the year has more feast days than market days, and the debt this incurs is itself traditional.
Miners and smiths; the underground is mapped better than the surface, and 'deep family' is the term for old money.
Nomadic in summer, settled in winter; the realm effectively has two capitals and two characters.
Storytellers who hold that a thing isn't true until it's been told well; chroniclers are licensed like publicans.
Quietly matriarchal; property passes daughter to daughter, and marrying in means marrying into her house.
Weavers and dyers; a person's cloth states their district, trade, and mourning at a glance, and lying by wardrobe is actionable.
Hard bargainers who consider the first price an insult ritually offered; markets are loud and friendships durable.
Foresters under old charters; every tree of size has a number, and the wardens who keep the rolls outrank the sheriffs.
Horse people; a family's standing is its stable, and giving a horse settles what money can't.
Stonemasons and bridge-builders; public works are signed like paintings, and a collapsed arch shames a family for a century.
Insular villages, each certain it alone does things properly; the realm unites instantly against outsiders and dissolves instantly after.
Music at everything — births, contracts, executions; a deal concluded without a tune is held to be unlucky and probably crooked.
Falconers and fowlers; the hunting-right on a marsh is a dowry, and poaching feuds outlast dynasties.
Practical polytheists; gods are hired like tradesmen for the job at hand, and no priest finds this offensive.
Bathhouse society; the day's real business concludes in the steam, and a magistrate who avoids the baths is presumed corrupt.
Salt-farmers on the coast, salt-carriers inland; the salt road is the spine of the realm and its slang the lingua franca.
Beekeepers and orchardists; hives are inherited separately from land, and hive-theft is tried as kidnapping.
Bureaucratic to a degree visitors find hallucinatory; there is a stamp for objecting to stamps, and it is used.
Wrestling is the civic sport and the civil court of last resort; champions are addressed by title for life.
Tattooed chronicles; a life's record is worn on the skin, and reading someone uninvited is a duel-worthy liberty.
Gardeners in everything; even the poorest door has its pot, and the phrase for peace is 'green years'.
Young realm, old grudges; half the nobility are first-generation and all the more exacting about precedence for it.
